*Includes people in prison for new offenses and with sentence lengths that are not less than one year or life sentences.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In South Dakota, by statute there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from the 25- to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. For crimes committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made nonparolable. A large percentage of people who are incarcerated are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission, with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.”¹
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, 35 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2013 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 21 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 100 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were Unknown people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 92 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 30 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 70 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 29 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (28%) and aggravated or simple assault (21%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, 37 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## State Notes Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Porgram (NCRP) for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY is treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000.

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state's code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows:First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on thehighest possiblepercentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who's most serious offense is non-violent, and 75 for those whose most serious offense is violent.and ). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in thereport or previous reports, it is used as the best estimate. Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent are missing PEY (relevant) cases exclude those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available.

Estimates for the percent of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percent of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percent of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies (technically, a mixed-effects model with random slopes for both variables). The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percent of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's departmet of corrections' report (2023).⁴ # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 2 percent, changing from 3,434 in 2010 to 3,353 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, 50 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2022, 85 percent of people in prison were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2022, 35 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drugs offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 33 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 10 percent, dropping from 3,253 in 2010 to 2,929 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 68 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 46 percent increase compared to 2013. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2020, 100 percent of prison releases were conditional releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Data is not available. South Dakota did not submit this data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2020.
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, 77 percent of prison releases were males. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, 41 percent of prison releases were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 49 percent of prison releases were for people incarcerated for drugs offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_black_South Dakota.png){width=1182} ::: ::: In 2020, Black people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 7.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in South Dakota. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_hispanic_South Dakota.png){width=1177} ::: ::: In 2020, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in South Dakota. ### Gender Gender disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
:::

``` ::: ::: ### Gender In 2020, females spent 0.6 years fewer on average in prison compared to males in South Dakota. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by gender in 2020. For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 4.5 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 1.8 more years in prison past parole eligibility on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.* :::
## End Notes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Bree Crye, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: South Dakota *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, August 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/south_dakota_doi_report_8_10_23.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/south_dakota_doi_report_8_10_23.pdf).

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., "American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report" (Minneapolis, Minnesoda: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, pp. 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see [pending].
